title: History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita
subtitle: Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]
caption:
History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita, 1000 to 2018

Unit 1 ‘The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]

Across more than a millennium, real gross domestic product per capita in selected countries remained near the bottom of the scale until the modern era, then rose steeply in the 19th and 20th centuries. The vertical axis measures income in 2011 US dollars adjusted for Purchasing Power Parity, ranging from $0 to $40,000. The horizontal axis spans the years 1000 to 2018. Five national trajectories are plotted: Japan (orange), United Kingdom (blue), Italy (green), China (purple), and India (black). From 1000 through roughly 1800, all five lines cluster tightly between $0 and $5,000, with minor fluctuations visible but no sustained upward movement. Italy’s line shows slight variation earlier than others, dipping below $3,000 around 1600 before recovering modestly by 1800. After 1800, the United Kingdom’s line begins a steady climb, crossing $10,000 around 1900 and reaching nearly $40,000 by 2018. Japan’s line remains flat until the late 1800s, then accelerates sharply after 1950, surpassing $35,000 by 2018. Italy’s line follows a similar late surge, peaking just under $35,000. China and India remain near the baseline until the mid-20th century; China’s line rises abruptly after 1980, reaching about $15,000 by 2018, while India’s line climbs more gradually, ending near $7,000. The units of measurement are 2011 US dollar which is used to compare Purchasing Power Parity and GDP across countries over time. Not all deaths in a country may have been registered with a cause of death — though this note does not apply to GDP data, it appears on the chart and must be acknowledged as printed. The most visible pattern is the prolonged flatness followed by an abrupt, steep rise in income per capita for all five nations, occurring at different times but converging toward high values only in the last two centuries.
